name: Watchdog (Python)
what_it_does: Cross-platform Python library for monitoring filesystem events — file creation, modification, deletion, and directory changes. Uses native OS APIs (inotify on Linux, FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows) with polling fallback for network filesystems. Used for hot-reload dev servers, file-triggered pipelines, and watch-based automation.
use_cases: Trigger agent workflows when new files arrive in a watched directory — process uploads, outputs, or data drops automatically
Build file-based event pipelines where agent actions are triggered by file system changes (new model checkpoints, data exports)
Implement hot-reload functionality in agent development environments that restart or reload configs when source files change
Monitor agent output directories and trigger downstream processing steps when output files are written
Watch configuration files for changes and apply live config updates to long-running agent processes without restart
not_for: Distributed file system monitoring across multiple hosts — Watchdog monitors local filesystem only; use S3 event notifications or cloud storage triggers for distributed monitoring
High-frequency file changes at scale — many rapid changes can overwhelm the event queue; debounce events for high-frequency write scenarios
Network filesystems without polling — inotify/FSEvents don't work on NFS/SMB; use polling observer for network mounts
best_when: You need cross-platform filesystem event monitoring in Python for local files — triggering actions on file changes without polling loops.
avoid_when: You're watching network-mounted filesystems or need distributed file event streaming — use cloud storage event notifications instead.

auth.notes: No authentication — local Python library. Uses OS filesystem permissions for access control.

pricing.notes: Watchdog is open source and free.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local execution. inotify queue size limits apply on Linux (default 8192 events).

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Observer thread exceptions are silently swallowed by default — must subclass or set unhandled_exception_handler to surface errors from event handlers.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: File system events are not idempotent — the same file modification produces a single event. Agents must handle event deduplication if the same file is processed multiple times across restarts.
known_agent_gotchas: FileCreatedEvent may fire before the file is fully written — use a small delay or watch for FileModifiedEvent to ensure file is complete before processing
On macOS with FSEvents, events are delivered in batches every ~1s — not true real-time; use polling observer for lower latency on macOS
inotify watch limits on Linux default to 8192 — monitoring deep directory trees can exhaust watches; increase fs.inotify.max_user_watches in /proc/sys/fs/inotify
ObservedWatch paths must exist at observer start time — watching a directory that doesn't exist yet requires polling or creating the directory first
Recursive=True on Windows uses polling for subdirectories > 2 levels deep and can miss events for deeply nested structures
Event handler exceptions crash the observer thread silently — always wrap handler logic in try/except and log errors to prevent silent failures

security_score_components.security_notes: Local filesystem only. Event handlers in agent pipelines should validate file paths to prevent path traversal if watch directories are user-controllable.
